The AgentCanvas framework is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 — a permissive license that allows commercial and private use, modification, and redistribution, with an explicit patent grant. The authoritative text is the LICENSE file at the repository root. §1 At a glance This is a plain-language summary, not a substitute for the full license text (see also apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0 ). ✓ Permissions ! Conditions ✗ Limitations Commercial use License & copyright notice Trademark use Modification State changes Liability Distribution Warranty Patent use Private use In short: you may use, modify, and distribute AgentCanvas (including commercially), provided you retain the license and copyright notice and state significant changes. The license grants patent rights from contributors but offers no warranty and no trademark rights. §2 Plugins carry their own license AgentCanvas follows a small-core + plugin-ecosystem model (see Contributing ). Research contributions are plugins — nodesets, graph presets, and graph nodes — that live in their authors' own git repositories and carry their own license and copyright . The Apache-2.0 license here covers the framework, not third-party plugins. Plugin authors choose a license appropriate for their code and retain full authorship — which is exactly what lets a paper cite its own implementation independently. Citing AgentCanvas Citation is decoupled from the version number — see the Versioning Policy §8 for how to cite a fixed snapshot (tag the cited version, mint a Zenodo DOI).

title: Auto-Memory Store
text: Auto-Memory Store How Claude Code's auto-memory is stored and managed in this repo — a symlinked, in-repo store, routed by topic, and shared selectively via git. Claude Code keeps a persistent, file-based auto-memory — one fact per markdown file, indexed by a MEMORY.md that is loaded into context at the start of every session. By default this store lives per-project under ~/.claude/ and never enters the repository. AgentCanvas overrides that in three ways: the store is relocated into the repo (so git can version it), routed into topic subdirectories instead of a flat folder, and shared selectively — project knowledge travels between machines, personal preferences stay local. The routing rules are the source of truth in CLAUDE.md § Memory Layout; this page explains the mechanism behind them. on this page The default · Our override · Directory layout · A memory file · The index · Shareability · Machine setup §1 The default: per-project, flat, frontmatter Out of the box, Claude Code's auto-memory is strictly per-project — there is no global, cross-project memory. Each project gets its own folder under ~/.claude/projects/<encoded-path>/memory/ , where the encoded path is the repo's absolute path with every non-alphanumeric character replaced by - . Inside that folder: One fact = one markdown file , written flat into the folder (no subdirectories). A MEMORY.md index lives alongside the files and is loaded into context each session — it is how the agent knows what memories exist. Four frontmatter types classify each file: user (who the user is), feedback (how to work), project (ongoing work / constraints), and reference (pointers to external resources). The filename prefix mirrors the type ( feedback_* , project_* , user_* ). Because it sits under ~/.claude/ , the default store is local to one machine and is never committed — switch machines and the memory is gone. §2 Our override: an in-repo store, reached by symlink AgentCanvas keeps its memory inside the repository at .claude/memory/ so it can be versioned and synced like any other file. Claude Code still reads from the per-project path it expects — the two are bridged by a symlink, set up once per machine ( §7 ): ~/.claude/projects/-data-ws-vln-vlnworkspace/memory # path Claude Code reads at session start | symlink v <repo>/.claude/memory/ # the real store — versioned inside the repo The symlink is transparent: Claude Code follows it when loading MEMORY.md and when surfacing a relevant memory mid-session, so every memory behaviour works unchanged — the bytes just happen to live in the repo. On top of relocation, this repo adds topic routing : instead of a flat folder, a new memory is dropped into the subdirectory that matches its topic, never the .claude/memory/ root. §3 Directory layout .claude/memory/ |-- MEMORY.md # the index — loaded into context every session |-- architect/ # AAS pipeline + experiment infra (tracked) |-- platform/ # agentcanvas framework semantics (tracked) |-- nodeset/ # specific nodeset / sim / scene facts (tracked) |-- eval/ # eval-suite quirks & metric facts (tracked) |-- research/ # paper / thesis / supervisor / style (tracked) |-- working-style/ # general collaboration norms (local-only) `-- user/ # user profile / env / preferences (local-only) Subdir What lives here Shared via git? architect/ AAS pipeline + experiment-infra rules, contract semantics Tracked platform/ agentcanvas framework facts — node / wire / state / runtime semantics, engine bugs Tracked nodeset/ specific nodeset / sim / scene facts (simpler, voxposer, smartway, hm3d, mp3d, …) Tracked eval/ eval-suite quirks and metric facts Tracked research/ paper / thesis / supervisor briefing / project identity / research-style feedback Tracked working-style/ general collaboration norms not specific to this repo Local-only user/ user profile / env / preferences Local-only §4 Anatomy of a memory file Each file is one fact: YAML frontmatter ( name , description , type , plus an originSessionId stamp) followed by the fact itself. For feedback and project memories the body carries a **Why:** line (what prompted the rule) and a **How to apply:** line (the concrete action), so a future session can act on it without re-deriving the context. --- name: Graph JSON is configuration of hidden Python code description: Don't describe graph JSON as "not configuration of hidden code" — it IS exactly that. type: project originSessionId: 98098be4-f739-4da1-a5c3-168a8f468ac3 --- A graph JSON is a configuration of hidden Python code. Each node `type` string resolves to a registered BaseCanvasNode subclass; the JSON supplies the wiring. **Why:** I previously framed graphs as "not a configuration of hidden code" — the user corrected this; the framing was wrong. **How to apply:** Frame a graph as "the complete declarative spec of an agent's topology, wiring, and config — node behaviour lives in registered Python classes." One fact per file. Memories are deliberately small and single-purpose. Before saving a new one, check for an existing file that already covers the topic and update it rather than spawning a near-duplicate; delete memories that turn out to be wrong. Don't store what the repo already records (code structure, past fixes, git history, CLAUDE.md ). §5 The index: MEMORY.md MEMORY.md is the only file loaded into context at session start — it is the table of contents the agent reads to know what it remembers; the individual files are read on demand (or surfaced automatically when relevant). Every memory gets one pointer line under a per-directory header. Because the routing is topic-based, the index uses one ## subdir/ — description header per directory, with subdir-prefixed links: ## platform/ — agentcanvas framework semantics (node / wire / state / runtime) - [ project_graph_is_config.md ]( platform/project_graph_is_config.md ) — Graph JSON IS configuration of hidden Python code; frame as "complete topology spec". The index is itself git-tracked (see below) and edited append-only. That fixes an earlier failure mode where a memory file synced across machines via git but its index line didn't — leaving the receiving machine unable to find a memory it physically had. Append-only edits also keep merge conflicts manageable. §6 Shareability: a per-directory .gitignore split Not all memory should travel. Project knowledge — framework semantics, nodeset facts, research framing — is worth carrying between machines; personal context — collaboration habits, the user's environment — is local business. The split is enforced in .gitignore : ignore the whole store, then whitelist the directories that should sync. .claude/memory/* !.claude/memory/MEMORY.md !.claude/memory/architect/ !.claude/memory/platform/ !.claude/memory/nodeset/ !.claude/memory/eval/ !.claude/memory/research/ So architect/ , platform/ , nodeset/ , eval/ , research/ , and MEMORY.md are committed and shared; working-style/ and user/ stay on the machine that wrote them. §7 Machine setup On a fresh clone, the in-repo store exists but the symlink Claude Code reads from does not yet. One idempotent script wires it: # once per machine, after cloning the repo bash scripts/dev/setup_memory_symlink.sh The script computes the encoded project path (every non-alphanumeric character → - , the rule Claude Code uses), creates ~/.claude/projects/<encoded>/ if missing, and links its memory to <repo>/.claude/memory . It is safe to re-run: if the link is already correct it exits cleanly, and if a real directory already sits at the target path (an older, machine-local memory store) it refuses to overwrite and asks you to merge or move it aside — memory is never silently clobbered. The script only wires the link; syncing the files between machines is git's job.

title: Pre-commit Hooks
text: Pre-commit Hooks The commit-time check suite that keeps the repository clean, formatted, secret-free, and conventionally committed. AgentCanvas uses pre-commit to run a fixed suite of checks on every commit. The single source of truth is .pre-commit-config.yaml at the repo root. Hooks run in the order hygiene → formatters → linters → security → commit-msg ; formatters run before linters so lint errors reflect real issues, not whitespace. on this page Install · Run · The hook suite · Notable config & exclusions §1 Install (once after clone) pip install pre-commit pre-commit install pre-commit install --hook-type commit-msg # enable the commit-msg hook The first pre-commit install wires the pre-commit git hook (runs on git commit over staged files). The second, --hook-type commit-msg , is required for the Conventional Commits check to fire on your commit message — without it, that hook silently never runs. §2 Run Hooks run automatically on git commit over the staged files. To check the whole repo on demand (do this before pushing): pre-commit run --all-files Dirty-tree note pre-commit stashes unstaged changes while it runs. On a heavily-dirty tree that stash can conflict with hook auto-fixes; if that happens, verify the staged set directly with pre-commit run --files <paths> and commit that exact set. Never --no-verify past a real hook failure. §3 The hook suite Stage Hook What it enforces Hygiene pre-commit-hooks v4.6.0 trailing-whitespace Strips trailing whitespace end-of-file-fixer Ensures exactly one trailing newline check-yaml YAML files parse check-json JSON files parse (JSONC .vscode/ excluded) check-added-large-files Blocks files > 500 KB unless path-excluded check-merge-conflict Blocks leftover conflict markers check-case-conflict Blocks Foo.py vs foo.py collisions debug-statements Blocks breakpoint() / pdb.set_trace() mixed-line-ending Normalizes to LF (matches .editorconfig ) detect-private-key Blocks accidentally committed SSH/RSA keys Python ruff v0.15.7 ruff-check --fix Lints and auto-fixes (config in ruff.toml ) ruff-format Formats Python Frontend prettier v4.0.0-alpha.8 prettier Formats agentcanvas/frontend/src/** .ts/.tsx/.css/.json Security gitleaks v8.18.4 gitleaks Scans staged content for hardcoded secrets Commit msg conventional-pre-commit v3.4.0 conventional-pre-commit Enforces Conventional Commits: feat fix refactor docs test chore perf ci §4 Notable config & exclusions Large-file cap. check-added-large-files uses --maxkb=500 . Curated large content is path-excluded: outputs/archive/ (compressed run traces), architect-site/reference/_archive/ (frozen reference), and assets/readme/ (README hero GIFs). Vendored code is skipped. The whitespace / EOF / debug-statement / line-ending hooks exclude third_party/ , workspace/nodesets/policy/policy_vla/(adapters|models|policies)/ , and workspace/nodesets/env/env_detany3d/_vendor/ — upstream code we don't reformat. JSONC tolerated. check-json excludes .vscode/ (JSON-with-comments by design) and the frontend package-lock.json . Secret scanning. Before going public, also scan the full history once: gitleaks detect --source . --log-opts = "--all" Conventional Commits runs only at the commit-msg stage, so it needs the separate --hook-type commit-msg install above. Commit subjects follow type(scope): description (lowercase, imperative, ≤72 chars) — see Contributing § PR format .

title: Versioning Policy
text: Versioning Policy How AgentCanvas releases are numbered, tagged, recorded, and cited. SemVer 2.0, with the version number driven by API stability — not by paper milestones. This is the engineering-level versioning contract for AgentCanvas — the mechanics of how versions are expressed in git and on the forge. The paradigm-level meaning of each major version (what v1 vs v2 actually covers ) lives in Major Versions ; this page says how those versions are mechanically numbered, cut, recorded, and cited. on this page Three concerns · Number format · Bump rules · Public API · v0.x · v1.0.0 · Release candidates · Citation · Tags & releases · Changelog & deprecation · Security · Cheatsheet §1 Three separate concerns — do not conflate Mature projects keep these on independent axes. Forcing one to track another (for example " v1.0 = the paper is out") couples things that have no reason to move together. Concern Question it answers Where it lives Policy (this page) How do we number, tag, and cut releases? This page Record What changed in each version? CHANGELOG.md + each GitHub Release body Citation How does a paper cite a fixed snapshot? CITATION.cff + Zenodo DOI The one rule that drives everything else The version number is driven by exactly one thing: the state of the public API . It is not driven by publication milestones, marketing, or the calendar. §2 Version number format Versions follow SemVer 2.0 — vMAJOR.MINOR.PATCH , with a leading v , and only -rc.N as a pre-release suffix. v MAJOR . MINOR . PATCH [ - rc . N ] ^ ^ mandatory 'v' prefix only -rc.N pre-release suffix is used Valid Invalid v0.1.0 0.1.0 — missing v prefix v0.7.3 v1.0 — missing patch component v1.0.0-rc.1 v1.0.0-alpha.1 / -beta.1 — only -rc is used v1.0.0 , v1.4.2 , v2.0.0-rc.1 v1.0.0+build.42 — no build metadata in tags The v prefix is a git convention (Linux kernel, Kubernetes, Go, Node.js). Package managers (pip, npm, cargo) strip it on consumption, so it does not leak into pyproject.toml / package.json version: fields — those carry the bare 0.1.0 . Single source of truth. The git tag is authoritative. The version field in pyproject.toml (and package.json , if published) is bumped in the same commit that precedes the tag and must equal the tag minus its v . No version string is hardcoded anywhere else. §3 SemVer bump rules Bump When Examples MAJOR Paradigm shift across the v1 ↔ v2 boundary in Major Versions — today this means introducing runtime topology mutation . (Post-1.0, also any backward-incompatible public-API break.) v1 → v2 MINOR Backward-compatible new capability: new nodeset, new env/method port, new framework feature, deployable/packaging polish, eval-stack additions. v1.1, v1.2, … PATCH Backward-compatible bug fix or non-functional cleanup. No new user-facing capability. v1.4.1, v1.4.2, … v1.x vs v2 routing. If a feature can be expressed with bounded, predeclared topology — even via router-and-bounded-pool, hidden-in-node, or K_max over-provisioning — it is v1.x . If it can exist only with runtime topology mutation, it is v2 . Any roadmap item marked "moved to v2" in roadmap stays out of the v1.x minor stream. §4 What the public API is SemVer protects the public API . For AgentCanvas that surface is explicitly: Graph JSON schema — the on-disk format of a saved agent (node / wire / port / state / config shape). A graph that loads on vX.Y must keep loading on every later vX.* . Nodeset author contracts — the BaseCanvasNode / BaseNodeSet base-class APIs that external nodeset authors subclass. CLI / HTTP surfaces — documented commands and backend endpoints used to run and evaluate graphs. Internal implementation (executor internals, private helpers, frontend wiring) is not part of the contract and may change in any release. When in doubt, a symbol is public only if it is documented as such. §5 v0.x — the pre-stable phase v0.x follows SemVer's v0 interpretation: no backward-compatibility guarantee between any two v0 versions, including patch bumps. External consumers should treat any v0.x pin as snapshot-only and expect to adapt when bumping. We still bump MINOR for new capability and PATCH for fixes (for human readability), but the SemVer compatibility contract does not yet bind. Breaking changes do not require a MAJOR bump and do not require a deprecation window — but they must be recorded in CHANGELOG.md under Changed / Removed . API churn from research iteration is expected here — which is precisely why we stay in v0.x until the surfaces in §4 settle. §6 v1.0.0 — meaning and ship criteria What v1.0.0 means The public API is frozen and we now commit to strict SemVer. It is a statement about the software , not about a publication. v1.0.0 ships when all of the following hold: Open-sourceable — public repository with LICENSE , install docs, and CONTRIBUTING . Coverage — the v1 "Agent forms covered" list in Major Versions §1 is each exercised by at least one canonical, runnable graph. API declared stable — the three public surfaces in §4 are documented and frozen under SemVer; MINOR/PATCH bumps from here must remain backward-compatible. Why the paper is not a v1.0 criterion A stable, open-sourced codebase can reach 1.0 whether or not a paper is in review; conversely, a paper may be submitted while the code is still legitimately v0.x . Tying the SemVer major to an external review timeline would make the version number — whose entire job is to predict compatibility — hostage to events unrelated to compatibility. The paper is handled on the citation axis (§8). §7 Release candidates The only pre-release suffix is -rc.N , and it carries a precise meaning: feature-complete for the target version, accepting only release-blocking bug fixes. When Tag Trigger Target version is feature-complete vX.0.0-rc.1 Code frozen except for fixes to release-blocking bugs Each subsequent stabilization round vX.0.0-rc.2 , … A round of fixes lands; re-cut for testing No release-blocking bugs remain vX.0.0 Final release Ordering follows SemVer precedence: v1.0.0-rc.1 < v1.0.0-rc.2 < v1.0.0 . We do not use -alpha / -beta : v0.x already covers "early / unstable", and -rc is reserved for the final stabilization window. §8 Citation & archival — the independent axis Academic citation is decoupled from the version number. Mechanism: CITATION.cff at the repo root. GitHub natively renders a "Cite this repository" button from it and exports BibTeX / APA. It carries authors, title, and (once minted) the DOI. Zenodo ↔ GitHub integration. Enabling it makes Zenodo mint a DOI for every GitHub Release automatically: one version DOI per release plus one concept DOI that always resolves to the latest version. This is the standard archival path for research software. When a paper is submitted or revised: Tag the then-current version — whatever the code state warrants (almost always a v0.x or a -rc ), not a forced v1.0.0 . Cut the matching GitHub Release; Zenodo mints the version DOI. Cite that version DOI in the paper, and record the citation in CITATION.cff . This gives the paper a permanent, reproducible snapshot to point at while keeping the version number an honest statement about API stability. §9 Tag mechanics & releases All tags are annotated (never lightweight — lightweight tags lose the tagger / date / message that git describe and the Release UI rely on): git tag -a v0.1.0 -m "v0.1.0 — first tagged snapshot" git push origin v0.1.0 Cut from master only. No tags on feature branches or stale commits. To patch a released version, commit the fix on master , then tag — we do not run parallel hotfix branches off old tags. Append-only. Never git tag -f , never force-push a tag. A mistaken tag is left in place and its deprecation announced in the next release's notes; rewriting history breaks anyone who pinned the old hash. Tag vs Release — two distinct concepts: Tag Release Owner git itself GitHub (the forge) What it is Immutable pointer to a commit hash Metadata wrapper: changelog text, assets, pre-release flag, DOI…

title: Running a Parallel Batch Eval
text: Running a Parallel Batch Eval Evaluate one graph against many episodes in parallel: K env subprocesses step concurrently while a single shared GPU policy server batches their inference calls. End-to-end walkthrough — from clicking Start on the Eval page to opting a custom GPU node into the batched-inference path. This is a tutorial. For how the worker pool is wired or why the policy server is shared instead of replicated, read Design — Batch Eval System (and ADR-eval-002 / ADR-eval-004). To drive this whole flow from a script or coding agent instead of the UI, see Driving the Backend from a Coding Agent . 1. Prerequisites Backend running on :8000 (your own session) or under /experiment:run (allocated $BACKEND_URL ); canvas UI on :5173 . A graph that uses an env nodeset — e.g. workspace/graphs/cma_baseline.json for env_habitat + policy_cma . The env's dataset already in place (Habitat-Sim split downloaded, policy checkpoint under data/policy/ , etc.). 2. First Parallel Run Open the Eval page in the UI. Pick your graph. Set Episode Count to e.g. 50 . Set Worker Count to 4 . Leave the rest at defaults. Click Start . What you should see: The progress strip splits into 4 rows, one per worker. Episodes complete in arbitrary order; the final summary.json sorts them back by episode_index . VRAM stays roughly flat as you raise worker_count — one policy server, K env subprocesses. If wall-clock doesn't drop ≈linearly, your bottleneck is the GPU forward, not the env step — § 4 covers turning that knob. 3. Tune the Per-Step Budget Each episode is capped at max_steps × per_step_budget_sec . A single slow step aborts that episode with error="timeout after Xs" — the worker resumes the queue, so one stuck episode never blocks the pool. Resolution order, highest precedence first: EvalConfig.per_step_budget_sec from the eval form. The env nodeset's default_per_step_budget_sec class var. BaseNodeSet.default_per_step_budget_sec = 5.0 s. Nodeset Default Rationale env_habitat 2.0 s Habitat-Sim step is sub-second; 2 s leaves GC headroom. Framework fallback 5.0 s Conservative for unknown nodesets. LLM-heavy graphs (MapGPT, NavGPT) 30.0 s Network round-trip + provider queueing. Override the budget when: Your graph runs an LLM call inside the loop and per-call latency varies a lot (rate limits, long prompts). You're profiling and want a tight budget so regressions abort fast. 4. Batch a Custom GPU Node Heavy GPU nodes (policy networks, local VLMs) should multiplex K parallel worker calls into one forward pass instead of being duplicated K times in VRAM. Three changes turn a normal node into a batched one. 4.1 Declare the opt-in class PolicyForwardTool(BaseCanvasNode): node_type = "policy_cma__forward" batched: ClassVar[bool] = True batch_dim: ClassVar[str] = "raw_obs" # which input port carries the per-sample slot input_ports = [ PortDef("raw_obs", "TEXT", "Raw observation"), PortDef("hidden_in", "ANY", "Prior RNN state", optional=True), ] output_ports = [ PortDef("action", "ACTION", "Predicted action"), PortDef("hidden_out", "ANY", "RNN state for next step"), ] Scanned at registration: if batched=True then batch_dim must name a real input port, or register_node() raises ValueError . 4.2 Move per-call state onto the wire The batched server is pure-functional — it holds no per-worker state. Anything that used to live on ctx (RNN hidden states, prior action, episode mask) becomes an explicit input port and an output port, fed back through the loop's IterIn / IterOut: policy.hidden_out ──► iterOut.hidden ──► (next iter) ──► iterIn.hidden ──► policy.hidden_in On iteration 0 the IterIn carries None (no upstream source on hidden from init ); the policy initialises zeros internally. 4.3 Implement the batched forward() The framework hands all K samples under a marker key; the node returns all K outputs under another: from app.server.batched_inference import OUTPUTS_KEY, SAMPLES_KEY async def forward(self, inputs, ctx): samples = inputs.get(SAMPLES_KEY) is_batched = isinstance(samples, list) if not is_batched: samples = [inputs] # local mode: degenerate batch of 1 # ... stack along batch_dim, call the model once, split outputs ... if is_batched: return {OUTPUTS_KEY: outputs} # list, one entry per sample return outputs[0] At worker_count=1 the queue still flushes after flush_timeout_ms (default 50 ms) with batch size 1 — adds a 50 ms tail per inference call but keeps the contract identical across all K. 4.4 Pitfalls Don't put state on ctx in a batched node. The handler is invoked once per flush, so K workers share one ctx . Use ports. Don't read module-level mutables from inside forward() — same shared-state trap, one level removed. Non-batched local nodes (e.g. basic_agent history accumulators) are unaffected because they're never multiplexed across workers. 5. Debug a Failing Episode Every EpisodeResult carries a worker_id (0..N-1) and owns a self-contained log dir under episodes/ep{NNNN}/ (ADR-eval-004) — reaching one episode's firings is a path, not a JSONL filter. 5.1 Inspect one episode's log # Episode 47's firings (file IS the episode): jq -c '.' outputs/eval_runs/<run_id>/episodes/ep0047/log.jsonl # Worker assignment across all episodes in the run: for ep in outputs/eval_runs/<run_id>/episodes/ep*/episode.json; do jq -r '"\(.episode_index)\tworker=\(.worker_id)\tstatus=\(.status)"' "$ep" done 5.2 Reproduce single-tenant If episode 47 errored on worker 2, narrow the run to just it: worker_count = 1 episode_count = 1 start_episode_index = 47 Same code path through _run_one_episode , no worker interleaving — easy to attach a debugger and read the logs linearly. 5.3 Watch progress live The Eval UI shows N progress strips when worker_count > 1 . The per_worker_status field on each eval_progress WebSocket frame also lists every worker's currently-running episode index — useful when you're driving the run from a script. 6. What This Doesn't Cover Topic Where to read Architecture — worker pool, batched-inference tier, override maps Design — Batch Eval System , ADR-eval-002 Per-episode log layout, replay parser ADR-eval-004 Driving evals from a coding agent / HTTP / MCP Driving the Backend from a Coding Agent Custom voluntary logging inside your nodes Using the Execution Log System Docker isolation per env subprocess Roadmap F7 (designed, not built) Per-node parallelism / Pregel supersteps Roadmap F3 (orthogonal axis) LLM-side dynamic batching (vLLM, etc.) v1 batches PyTorch policies only; LLM concurrency stays at litellm request-level Multi-graph A/B in one run Blocked by _current_run singleton in api/execution/eval.py — separate design Changelog 2026-05-18: Rewritten as a tutorial — design-doc material moved to Design — Batch Eval System ; sections re-shaped around tasks (prerequisites → run → tune → batch a node → debug → pointers). Title sanitised to drop the recycled &… escape chain. 2026-05-15: § Debugging rewritten for per-episode log dirs (ADR-eval-004). 2026-04-18: Initial — covered ADR-eval-002 Phase A/B/C end-user surface.

title: Driving the Backend from a Coding Agent
text: Driving the Backend from a Coding Agent You have an AgentCanvas backend, you have a coding agent (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.), and you want the agent to run experiments : kick off batch evals, watch progress, pull metrics, iterate on the graph. There are three programmatic surfaces today; this tutorial walks each one end-to-end so you can pick. Just want to click "Start" yourself? This tutorial is for programmatic / agent-driven use. For UI-driven evals (and the worker-pool / batched-inference knobs that apply to all three modes below), see Running a Parallel Batch Eval . For the design of these surfaces — endpoint contracts, MCP slot pool, PDEATHSIG crash cleanup — see Design — Coding-Agent Backend Surface . Mode Surface Lifecycle Best when A. /experiment:run Slash-skill submits one graph to the JobScheduler over HTTP Backend is yours on :8000 and stays up; skill submits, polls, prints metrics on exit. Companion: /experiment:status , /experiment:teardown One-off graph eval, hyperparameter sweep, ad-hoc smoke test against your already-running backend with GPU admission control B. MCP server ( agentcanvas-backend ) Typed MCP tools — eval_start , eval_status , eval_export , eval_stop , graph_list , eval_runs_list One backend per coding-agent conversation, isolated via a flock- based 5-slot pool on 8765-8769 ; PDEATHSIG-armed against crashes; backend self-exits when the conversation ends You want conversational backend driving without owning a :8000 backend yourself — debugging, ad-hoc queries, "run X, now compare with Y" C. /architect:<variant>:loop (AAS family) Per-variant loop skill drives proposer → implementer → evaluator; evaluator internally calls /experiment:run Variant owns an iter-tree under outputs/design_runs/<variant>/<task>/... with snapshots, bootstrap CI, archive.jsonl. Eval-step lifecycle delegated to A You're inside an architecture-search loop and want every cycle to land in a versioned iter dir with reproducible metrics All three surfaces drive the same backend code (FastAPI app under agentcanvas/backend/app/ ). They differ in how the agent addresses it and how much policy / bookkeeping the skill layers on top . 1. Pick a Mode You're about to… Use Run a graph once to confirm a bug fix worked /experiment:run (Mode A) Pull a finished run's metrics for analysis MCP eval_export (Mode B) or read outputs/eval_runs/{run_id}/summary.json Conversationally explore: "run X, what's SR, now try Y" MCP (Mode B) Compare two graph variants on a pinned episode subset MCP eval_start with episode_indices=[...] , called twice Start a new architecture-search loop (will produce 5+ iters) /architect:<variant>:loop (Mode C) Detach from a running submission and poll later /experiment:status <run_id> --watch Cancel everything in the scheduler before restarting /experiment:teardown 2. Mode A — /experiment:run Foreground submitter to the backend's JobScheduler (M1 design, 2026-05-07). Multiple jobs queue and run concurrently when their VRAM declarations fit; Ctrl-C posts a cancel and exits. Replaces the legacy /architect:experiment-http . 2.1 Prerequisites Backend up at :8000 (yours — start it once with the usual cd agentcanvas/backend && uvicorn app.main:app --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8000 or its auto_host wrapper). The skill never spawns a backend. A profile in .claude/experiment/profiles.yaml matching your workload's VRAM + GPU declarations. Unknown names fall back to defaults with a stderr warning. A graph at workspace/graphs/{name}.json . Optional companion {name}.exp.yaml for per-graph eval defaults. 2.2 Run /experiment:run mapgpt-mp3d mapgpt_mp3d episode_count=10 worker_count=2 Arguments: mapgpt-mp3d — profile key in profiles.yaml (GPU admission rule). mapgpt_mp3d — graph name under workspace/graphs/ (no extension). key=value overrides — common keys: episode_count , worker_count , step_budget , per_step_budget_sec , dataset , split , start_episode_index . Optional --workspace=<abs-path> overlay — let the backend pick up an architect iter's active_workspace/ without dirtying frozen workspace/ : /experiment:run mapgpt-mp3d mapgpt_mp3d \ --workspace=/path/to/vlnworkspace/outputs/design_runs/myloop/mapgpt_mp3d/v0/iter_3/active_workspace \ episode_count=10 2.3 What Happens Read profiles.yaml → build payload (eval block + scheduling block with marginal_vram_mb , exclusive_gpu ). POST /api/eval/v2/start with via_subprocess=true → returns {"run_id": "...", "initial_state": "pending"} . Poll GET /api/eval/v2/runs/{run_id} until the run reaches a terminal state ( done , error , aborted , cancelled ). On non-success, dump the tail of stderr.log markers from the scheduler subprocess so the failure isn't silent (see memory feedback_experiment_run_silent_failures ). Print final metrics and exit. 2.4 Detach and Poll Later If you Ctrl-C the foreground submitter without intent to cancel , the run continues on the backend. Reattach with the printed run_id: /experiment:status # queue + running snapshot /experiment:status <run_id> # one run, one shot /experiment:status <run_id> --watch # poll until terminal /experiment:status <run_id> --cancel # POST cancel and exit And to drain the whole queue (without touching the backend process — which is yours): /experiment:teardown 2.5 Output Results land in the backend-owned per-run dir, identified by the returned run_id : outputs/eval_runs/{run_id}/ summary.json aggregate metrics (written at completion) episodes/ep{NNNN}/ episode.json per-episode result (worker_id, status, metrics) log.jsonl this episode's firings assets/ screenshots, intermediate dumps stdout.log / stderr.log scheduler subprocess output No iter-tree, no trace.md , no token-cost rollup — those belong to Mode C, which wraps Mode A. 3. Mode B — MCP Server (raw) The MCP server ( agentcanvas/mcp_server/ ) packages the eval API as Model Context Protocol tools — the coding agent talks to the backend through typed tools with JSON-Schema-validated inputs , not curl. The server itself encapsulates pool-slot acquisition on 127.0.0.1:8765-8769 plus PDEATHSIG-armed crash cleanup — the agent never sees a port number, a PGID, or a signal handler. The full tool set (6 tools, all in agentcanvas/mcp_server/server.py ): Tool What it does eval_start Start a batch eval. Accepts graph_name , episode_count , worker_count , step_budget , episode_indices , etc. eval_status Live progress + currently-running episode per worker eval_export Read outputs/eval_runs/{run_id}/summary.json after completion — backend-independent eval_stop Cancel the live run on this conversation's backend graph_list Enumerate graphs in workspace/graphs/ with their workspace/architect/exp_profiles/{graph}.yaml profile fields eval_runs_list Newest-first listing of past runs from disk — works without a live backend 3.1 Prerequisites Mode A prerequisites minus the running backend — the MCP server spawns its own. mcp Python package in the agentcanvas conda env (already in agentcanvas/backend/requirements.txt ). Claude Code CLI on $PATH ( claude command) for registration. 3.2 Register Once per machine: claude mcp add agentcanvas-backend \ --scope local \ -e PYTHONPATH=/path/to/vlnworkspace/agentcanvas \ -- conda run -n agentcanvas --no-capture-output python -m mcp_server Flag Why --scope local This user, this project only. Use --scope user to share across all your projects, --scope project to commit a .mcp.json for the team. -e PYTHONPATH=… claude mcp add has no --cwd ; PYTHONPATH is the cleanest way to let python -m mcp_server find the package. --no-capture-output Critical — without it, conda buffers stdout and breaks MCP JSON-RPC framing. conda run -n agentcanvas Pin the env so the mcp package resolves correctly. Verify: claude mcp list | grep agentcanvas-backend # → agentcanvas-backend: conda run -n agentcanvas ... ✓ Connected Restart Claude Code after registration — MCP tool catalogs load once at session start, not hot-refreshed. 3.3 Use In any Claude Code session, just ask: List the available agent graphs. Claude calls…

title: Component Cookbook
text: Component Cookbook A progressive, hands-on tutorial for building custom components in AgentCanvas. Each chapter builds on the previous — start at Chapter 1 and stop wherever you have enough. Every code example is copy-pasteable and works with the auto-discovery system. Companion code : workspace/nodesets/common/example.py contains working implementations of every node pattern in this tutorial (prefixed example__ instead of tutorial__ ). Load with POST /api/components/nodesets/example/load . The inline code uses a tutorial__ prefix to teach the naming convention — adapt the prefix to your own nodeset name. Spec-level companions. This tutorial walks the API through worked examples. For the type signatures, lifecycle ordering, and design rationale behind every concept here, read: Design — Base Canvas Node — kind / ports / config / UI / execution / lifecycle Design — NodeSets — local vs server (auto-hosted), env 3-tier contract, manager + REST Design — State Containers (used by § 13) · Graph Executor (used by § 12) · Loop Control System Table of Contents Part I — Node Authoring (start here) Your First Node Configuring Nodes Node UI Customization Live Display Fields Voluntary Logging Dynamic Ports Grouping into a NodeSet Part II — Deployment Server Mode Env Metadata for Evaluation Part III — Graph Composition Building Graphs Composites and Graph Nodes Dataflow Loops State Containers Part IV — Policies Wrapping a Neural Policy Appendix A. Quick Reference 1. Your First Node A canvas node is a Python class that inherits from BaseCanvasNode . It declares its identity, ports, and implements forward() . That's it — the framework handles rendering, wiring, and execution. 1.1 Minimal Example Create a file at workspace/nodesets/my_first.py : from __future__ import annotations from typing import Any , ClassVar from app.components import BaseCanvasNode , BaseNodeSet , PortDef class GreetNode ( BaseCanvasNode ): """Generate a greeting message.""" node_type = "tutorial__greet" display_name = "Greet" description = "Generate a greeting for a given name" category = "tutorial" icon = "Hand" input_ports : ClassVar [ list ] = [ PortDef ( "name" , "TEXT" , "Person's name" ), ] output_ports : ClassVar [ list ] = [ PortDef ( "greeting" , "TEXT" , "The greeting message" ), ] async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : name = inputs . get ( "name" , "World" ) return { "greeting" : f "Hello, { name } !" } class MyFirstNodeSet ( BaseNodeSet ): name = "tutorial" description = "Tutorial companion nodes" def get_tools ( self ) -> list : return [ GreetNode ()] 1.2 What Each Part Does Attribute Purpose Convention node_type Unique identifier in graph JSON {nodeset}__{function} (double underscore) display_name Human label in sidebar catalog Short, descriptive description Tooltip and search text One sentence category Sidebar grouping "environment" , "tool" , "reasoning" , "control" , "output" , or custom icon Lucide icon name PascalCase (e.g. "ScanEye" , "Globe" ) input_ports What data the node receives List of PortDef output_ports What data the node produces List of PortDef forward() The node's logic Async, receives inputs dict, returns outputs dict 1.3 PortDef and Wire Types Each port is declared with PortDef(name, wire_type, description, optional) : PortDef ( "image" , "IMAGE" , "RGB observation" , optional = False ) # required by default PortDef ( "hint" , "TEXT" , "Optional hint" , optional = True ) # won't block execution Available wire types: Wire Type Python Type Use For TEXT str Strings, JSON, prompts IMAGE np.ndarray (H×W×3 uint8) RGB images DEPTH np.ndarray (H×W float32) Depth maps ACTION int Discrete actions (0=STOP, 1=FWD, 2=LEFT, 3=RIGHT) STATE dict Agent state (position, orientation) BOOL bool Flags, conditions METRICS dict Evaluation metrics (SPL, SR, etc.) OBSERVATION dict Full observation bundle STEP_RESULT dict Step output (obs + reward + done + info) 1.4 Auto-Discovery Drop your file in workspace/nodesets/ and the backend discovers it at startup. No registration code needed — WorkspaceComponentRegistry scans for all BaseNodeSet subclasses. To load at runtime without restarting: curl -X POST "http://localhost:8000/api/components/nodesets/tutorial/load" After loading, your node appears in the sidebar catalog under its category . 1.5 Entry Nodes (No Inputs) A node with an empty input_ports list is an entry node — it fires first without waiting for upstream data: class TimestampNode ( BaseCanvasNode ): node_type = "tutorial__timestamp" display_name = "Current Time" description = "Output the current timestamp" category = "tutorial" icon = "Clock" input_ports : ClassVar [ list ] = [] # ← entry node output_ports : ClassVar [ list ] = [ PortDef ( "time" , "TEXT" , "ISO timestamp" ), ] async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : from datetime import datetime , timezone return { "time" : datetime . now ( timezone . utc ) . isoformat ()} 2. Configuring Nodes Nodes often need user-editable settings — a temperature slider, a model selector, a text template. ConfigField renders inline controls directly on the node UI, no custom .tsx needed. 2.1 Adding Config Fields from app.components import BaseCanvasNode , ConfigField , NodeUIConfig , PortDef class TemperatureTextNode ( BaseCanvasNode ): node_type = "tutorial__temp_text" display_name = "Temperature Text" description = "Convert a temperature value to descriptive text" category = "tutorial" icon = "Thermometer" input_ports = [ PortDef ( "value" , "TEXT" , "Temperature in Celsius" )] output_ports = [ PortDef ( "description" , "TEXT" , "Descriptive text" )] ui_config = NodeUIConfig ( color = "orange" , config_fields = [ ConfigField ( "unit" , "select" , label = "Unit" , options = [ { "value" : "celsius" , "label" : "Celsius" }, { "value" : "fahrenheit" , "label" : "Fahrenheit" }, ], default = "celsius" ), ConfigField ( "threshold" , "slider" , label = "Hot threshold" , default = 30 , min = 0 , max = 50 , step = 1 ), ConfigField ( "verbose" , "toggle" , label = "Verbose output" , default = False ), ], ) async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : temp = float ( inputs . get ( "value" , "0" )) unit = self . config . get ( "unit" , "celsius" ) threshold = self . config . get ( "threshold" , 30 ) verbose = self . config . get ( "verbose" , False ) if unit == "fahrenheit" : temp = ( temp - 32 ) * 5 / 9 # convert to Celsius for comparison if temp >= threshold : label = "hot" elif temp >= threshold - 15 : label = "mild" else : label = "cold" if verbose : return { "description" : f " { temp : .1f } °C is { label } (threshold: { threshold } °C)" } return { "description" : label } 2.2 All Six Field Types field_type Widget Key Parameters Example "label" Read-only text default ConfigField("version", "label", label="Version", default="1.0") "text" Single-line input placeholder , default ConfigField("api_key", "text", label="API Key", placeholder="sk-...") "textarea" Multi-line input placeholder , default ConfigField("prompt", "textarea", label="Prompt", default="Describe...") "slider" Range slider min , max , step , default ConfigField("temp", "slider", label="Temperature", default=0.7, min=0, max=2, step=0.1) "select" Dropdown options , default ConfigField("model", "select", label="Model", options=[{"value":"gpt4","label":"GPT-4"}]) "toggle" Checkbox/switch default ConfigField("debug", "toggle", label="Debug mode", default=False) 2.3 Reading Config in forward() Config values live in self.config — a plain dict populated from the graph JSON before execution: async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : # Always provide defaults — config may be empty for new nodes model = self . config . get ( "model" , "gpt-4" ) temperature = float ( self . config . get ( "temperature" , 0.7 )) max_tokens = int ( self . config . get ( "max_tokens" , 256 )) ... The user changes these values on the canvas node UI. The values persist in the graph JSON under node.config . 3. Node UI…

title: Contributing a NodeSet
text: Contributing a NodeSet The fastest way to extend AgentCanvas is to write a nodeset — a group of canvas nodes with a shared lifecycle — or compose a graph out of existing nodesets, and contribute it by PR into workspace/ . This page walks a nodeset end-to-end: what can be one, the file template, the rules, local testing, how to open the PR, and how your work gets credited. For core framework work instead (fix a bug, extend an executor, improve the canvas UI), see CONTRIBUTING.md — open a Discussion first for anything big. 1. How contributing a nodeset works You add your files under workspace/ , AgentCanvas's WorkspaceComponentRegistry auto-discovers them at startup, and you open a PR. Three things make this low-friction: 1.1 Light review Content contributions get a light review: your nodeset loads, follows the component conventions, and stays inside workspace/ with no edits to the framework. There's no architectural gate — the heavy review is reserved for core changes that touch the plugin contract every nodeset author depends on. 1.2 You keep the credit Every nodeset and graph is attributed to its author/maintainer on the Credits board — maintained in the README and a dedicated doc-site page — so contributing into the shared repo, rather than a separate one, doesn't cost you authorship. If your contribution is part of a paper, its citation link goes on the Credits board too. 1.3 It versions with the repo Because your nodeset lives in the repo, it moves with the framework — when the platform refactors, your code is migrated alongside everything else, and breaking changes to the public API ( BaseCanvasNode , BaseNodeSet , PortDef , wire types) only land through an ADR . You don't have to track a moving target from the outside. 2. What you can contribute Any of the three extension types that WorkspaceComponentRegistry auto-discovers from workspace/ : Type What it is Lives in NodeSet A BaseNodeSet subclass with its canvas nodes — a loadable group of tools with shared lifecycle (e.g. Habitat env, SAM segmentation, a custom memory system) workspace/nodesets/*.py Graph A GraphDefinition JSON saved as an editable template ( kind="graph" ) or frozen composite ( kind="node" ) workspace/graphs/*.json or workspace/graph_nodes/*.json Search operator A BaseSearchOperator subclass implementing an Agent Architecture Search algorithm (e.g. evolutionary, MCTS, RL-based) workspace/search_operators/*.py — see Writing a Search Operator This tutorial focuses on nodesets — the most-used extension point with the most-stable API. A tool nodeset wraps a capability into nodes (a reconstructor, a SLAM system, a detector, a simulator); a method nodeset encodes an agent's reasoning (a NavGPT or MapGPT loop). For search operators, read Writing a Search Operator — that surface ships but its public API is still experimental. 3. Minimum viable nodeset A minimum viable nodeset is a single Python file under workspace/nodesets/<role>/ ( env / method / model / policy / common — see ADR-platform-008 ). That's the entire mechanism: workspace/nodesets/common/my_nodeset.py # the actual nodeset code (one file) Drop the file in, restart the backend (or POST /api/components/reload ), and WorkspaceComponentRegistry auto-discovers the BaseNodeSet subclass on startup — the nodes appear in the sidebar. No package registry, no install script, no build step. Research code is read, modified, and cited: a file next to the user's own graphs in a familiar working tree lowers the friction of "I want to tweak one line for my experiment" to near zero. 4. Writing a nodeset This section is the packaging surface. For the authoring API itself — every ClassVar , every UI knob, every lifecycle hook — see the worked-example tutorial Component Cookbook and the spec-level docs Base Canvas Node and NodeSets . 4.1 File template Every nodeset starts from this skeleton (matches workspace/nodesets/common/example.py ): # my_nodeset.py from __future__ import annotations from typing import Any , ClassVar from app.components import ( BaseCanvasNode , BaseNodeSet , ConfigField , NodeUIConfig , PortDef , ) class MyTool ( BaseCanvasNode ): """A single canvas node — appears in the sidebar, wires to other nodes.""" node_type = "my_nodeset__my_tool" # convention: nodeset__node display_name = "My Tool" description = "Does one useful thing" category = "example" # sidebar grouping icon = "Wand2" # lucide-react icon name input_ports : ClassVar [ list ] = [ PortDef ( "text" , "TEXT" , "Input text" )] output_ports : ClassVar [ list ] = [ PortDef ( "result" , "TEXT" , "Output text" )] ui_config : ClassVar [ NodeUIConfig ] = NodeUIConfig ( color = "sky" , config_fields = [ ConfigField ( "suffix" , "text" , label = "Suffix" , default = "!" ), ], ) async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : text = inputs . get ( "text" , "" ) suffix = self . config . get ( "suffix" , "!" ) self . _self_log ( "input_length" , len ( text )) return { "result" : text . upper () + suffix } class MyNodeSet ( BaseNodeSet ): """The NodeSet class — groups tools with a shared lifecycle.""" name = "my_nodeset" description = "My custom tools for AgentCanvas" def get_tools ( self ) -> list : return [ MyTool ()] 4.2 The 5 universal rules from __future__ import annotations at the top of every file — required for Python 3.8 compatibility in the ac-vlnce env and for forward references. One NodeSet per file : all the node classes live in the same file as the BaseNodeSet subclass, declared above it. node_type naming convention : "<nodeset_name>__<node_name>" (double underscore separator). Pick a nodeset name that's unlikely to collide with others. ClassVar[...] annotations : input_ports , output_ports , and ui_config must be declared with ClassVar to prevent Python from treating them as instance attributes. Wire types are fixed : use one of IMAGE , DEPTH , ACTION , STATE , TEXT , BOOL , METRICS , OBSERVATION , STEP_RESULT , ANY . No custom wire types in a nodeset (register via a core PR + ADR if you genuinely need a new one). 4.3 What you can and cannot import Can import (stable public API): from app.components import BaseCanvasNode, BaseNodeSet, PortDef, NodeUIConfig, ConfigField, DisplayField from app.standard.wire_types import IMAGE, DEPTH, ACTION, ... (or the string literals directly) Any Python standard library, any PyPI package (declare your extra dependencies in the nodeset's README or its env) Habitat, habitat-sim, torch, transformers, numpy, PIL — these are framework-external and nodeset code may use them freely Cannot import (unstable internal APIs, subject to breakage): Anything under app.agent_loop.* — the executor internals may refactor app.graph_def internals — use the types only through BaseCanvasNode.forward() signature Private modules (anything with _ prefix) Framework import boundary : the core framework ( agentcanvas/backend/app/ ) is forbidden from importing domain libraries like habitat . Your nodeset, living in workspace/nodesets/ , is on the correct side of that boundary — feel free to import whatever simulators or ML libraries you need. 4.4 Testing locally Before opening the PR, verify the nodeset loads and runs: # Your file lives at workspace/nodesets/<role>/my_nodeset.py — start the backend cd /path/to/agentcanvas-checkout/ bash agentcanvas/run_dev.sh # Check that your NodeSet appears curl http://localhost:8000/api/components/nodesets | jq '.[] | .name' If your NodeSet shows up in the list, it's loaded. If not, check the backend log — import errors are printed at startup. Then open http://localhost:5173, drag your node from the sidebar into a fresh graph, wire it to an input and an output, and press Play. If it produces the expected output, you're ready to open the PR. 5. Opening the PR Open a PR against master adding your files under workspace/ . Keep it to one concern, and make sure the local checks pass ( pre-commit run --all-files , backend pytest including the import-boundary test). See CONTRIBUTING § PR format for the…

title: Tutorial: Using the Execution Log System
text: Tutorial: Using the Execution Log System The execution log system (ADR-observability-003) records per-node I/O for every firing during graph execution. It captures what went in , what came out , and how long it took — automatically, for every node. Nodes can also emit voluntary logs with domain-specific details (LLM prompts, token counts, parsed actions) that the executor cannot see from the outside. This tutorial walks through adding logging to a custom nodeset, running a graph, and inspecting the results via REST API, JSONL files, and the frontend Log Viewer. Reference : For the full system specification, see Execution Logs . 1. Prerequisites The agentcanvas conda environment activated The AgentCanvas backend running ( cd agentcanvas && bash run_dev.sh ) Familiarity with BaseCanvasNode and NodeSets 2. How It Works The log system has two layers: ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Node forward() │ │ │ │ self._self_log("prompt", prompt) ◄── Interior │ │ self._self_log("tokens", 142) (opt-in) │ │ return {"action": 1} │ │ │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ GraphExecutor (wraps every firing) │ │ │ │ inputs = {port: value} ◄── Exterior │ │ outputs = {port: value} (auto) │ │ duration_ms = 25.4 │ │ port_wire_types = {port: "TEXT"} │ │ │ │ → log_node() → ring buffer + JSONL │ │ → exec_log WS event → frontend │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ Exterior layer — automatic, zero effort. The executor records port data, timing, and wire types for every firing. Interior layer — opt-in. Nodes call self._self_log(key, value) inside forward() to record domain-specific data. These entries appear in the inner_log array of the log entry. 3. Building a Logged NodeSet This tutorial ships with a ready-to-run example: NodeSet : workspace/nodesets/example.py — five nodes with different logging patterns Graph : workspace/graphs/text_analysis_demo.json — a DAG wiring five custom nodes + two in-graph viewers No external dependencies beyond numpy. The nodeset is auto-discovered by WorkspaceComponentRegistry at startup. 3.1 The NodeSet File Open workspace/nodesets/example.py to see the full implementation. Here are the key highlights: TextSource — Entry node (no inputs). Outputs configurable text from a textarea config field: class TextSource ( BaseCanvasNode ): node_type = "example__source" input_ports : ClassVar [ list ] = [] # no inputs → fires first as seed output_ports : ClassVar [ list ] = [ PortDef ( "text" , "TEXT" , "The configured text" )] async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : text = self . config . get ( "text" , "default text..." ) self . _self_log ( "text_length" , len ( text )) self . _self_log ( "preview" , text [: 80 ]) return { "text" : text } WordCounter — Logs simple scalars: async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : text = inputs . get ( "text" , "" ) words = text . split () unique = set ( w . lower () for w in words ) self . _self_log ( "word_count" , len ( words )) # scalar int self . _self_log ( "unique_words" , len ( unique )) # scalar int self . _self_log ( "avg_word_length" , round ( # scalar float sum ( len ( w ) for w in words ) / max ( len ( words ), 1 ), 1 , )) return { "count" : str ( len ( words )), "text" : text } SentimentTag — Logs a structured dict: async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : # ... keyword matching ... self . _self_log ( "analysis" , { # structured dict "positive_matches" : sorted ( pos_hits ), "negative_matches" : sorted ( neg_hits ), "positive_score" : len ( pos_hits ), "negative_score" : len ( neg_hits ), "result" : sentiment , }) return { "sentiment" : sentiment , "text" : text } TextSummary — Logs multiple entries to trace the pipeline: async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : # ... self . _self_log ( "input_lengths" , { "text" : len ( text ), ... }) # entry 1 self . _self_log ( "format_mode" , fmt ) # entry 2 # ... build summary ... self . _self_log ( "output_length" , len ( summary )) # entry 3 return { "summary" : summary } RandomTriangleImage — Generates an IMAGE and logs shape/color metadata: class RandomTriangleImage ( BaseCanvasNode ): node_type = "example__random_triangle" output_ports : ClassVar [ list ] = [ PortDef ( "rgb" , "IMAGE" , "Generated RGB image" )] async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : w = int ( self . config . get ( "width" , 320 )) h = int ( self . config . get ( "height" , 240 )) img = np . full (( h , w , 3 ), 255 , dtype = np . uint8 ) color = [ random . randint ( 30 , 230 ) for _ in range ( 3 )] pts = [( random . randint ( 0 , w - 1 ), random . randint ( 0 , h - 1 )) for _ in range ( 3 )] _fill_triangle ( img , pts , color ) self . _self_log ( "image_shape" , list ( img . shape )) # asset logging trigger self . _self_log ( "triangle_color" , color ) self . _self_log ( "vertices" , pts ) return { "rgb" : img } # IMAGE wire type → saved as sidecar JPEG Because the output port has wire type IMAGE , the executor's log_serialize_with_assets() automatically saves the numpy array as a JPEG file in outputs/runs/{id}/assets/ — no extra code needed. 3.2 In-Graph Viewers The graph also includes two built-in viewer nodes — sink nodes that display data directly on the canvas during execution: Viewer Node Type Input Port What It Shows Observation Viewer imageViewer rgb (IMAGE) Renders the generated triangle image inline on the canvas Scroll Text textScroll text (TEXT) Accumulates summaries across iterations as a scrollable stack on the canvas Viewer nodes are built into the framework ( graph_executor.py ). They have no output ports — they consume data and display it. Wire any IMAGE or TEXT port to them to get live visual feedback without leaving the canvas. 3.3 Key Patterns Demonstrated Node Logging Pattern What It Shows TextSource _self_log("key", scalar) Entry node with config-driven output WordCounter _self_log("key", scalar) Simple key-value pairs (numbers, strings) SentimentTag _self_log("key", dict) Structured analysis data TextSummary Multiple _self_log() calls Step-by-step pipeline tracing RandomTriangleImage _self_log("key", list) IMAGE wire type with sidecar asset logging All five nodes also get automatic exterior logging for free — the executor captures their inputs , outputs , duration_ms , and port_wire_types without any code in the nodes. The two viewer nodes also appear in the log — they log received_ports . 4. Running and Generating Logs 4.1 Start the Dashboard cd agentcanvas && bash run_dev.sh # Backend: http://localhost:8000 (Swagger: /docs) # Frontend: http://localhost:5173 The example nodeset is auto-discovered at startup from workspace/nodesets/ . You can verify via API: curl -s "localhost:8000/api/components/nodesets" | python -m json.tool 4.2 Open and Run the Graph The graph file workspace/graphs/text_analysis_demo.json wires five custom nodes + two built-in viewers into a DAG: ┌─── count ──────────────┐ │ ▼ [Text Source]─── text ──►[Word Counter] [Text Summary]──►[Summary Viewer] └── text ──►[Sentiment ]─ sentiment ► (textScroll) Tagger ─── text ────► [Random Triangle]── rgb ──►[Image Viewer] (imageViewer) Steps to run: Open the frontend at http://localhost:5173 In the Explorer sidebar (left), expand the Graphs section Click Text Analysis Demo to open it in the canvas Seven nodes appear on the canvas — five custom nodes + two viewer sinks Optionally, click the Text Source node and edit the text in its config textarea Click the Play button (▶) in the ExecutionToolbar Watch all nodes fire — the Image Viewer shows a random colored triangle, and the Summary Viewer shows the analysis text inline on the canvas The graph runs, the executor captures logs automatically, and you're ready to inspect them. 4.3 View Logs in the Log Viewer After execution completes: Navigate to the Log Viewer page (click the Logs icon in the header, or click "Open" in the OutputDrawer's Logs tab) The sidebar lists your…

title: Tutorial: Creating the Habitat NodeSet
text: Tutorial: Creating the Habitat NodeSet This tutorial walks through wrapping the Habitat-Sim environment as a NodeSet — a loadable group of canvas-node tools. We show two approaches : Way 1: Local — everything runs in the same process and conda env Way 2: Auto-Hosted Server — same code, deployed in a separate process automatically (ADR-server-001) Both produce identical canvas nodes. The nodeset code is the same — the only difference is how you load it. Note : For non-Python servers (C++, Rust) or custom protocols, the manual ServerNodeSet + ServerApp pattern still exists. This tutorial covers the common case: Python nodesets. 1. Prerequisites Habitat-Sim installed in the ac-vlnce conda env (Python 3.8; the habitat-sim wheel is binary-locked to that version). The production nodeset already exists at workspace/nodesets/env/env_habitat.py — it bundles HabitatEnvManager (the engine), the canvas tool classes, and EnvHabitatNodeSet in one file. This tutorial walks a simplified pedagogical version; the real file is ~1700 lines with extras covered in § 5.3. Understanding of Wire Types and NodeSets (especially § 5 Env Nodeset Contract ). 2. What We're Building Five canvas nodes for the Habitat environment: Node Ports In Ports Out Purpose observe (none) rgb (IMAGE), depth (DEPTH) Get current view step action (ACTION) rgb, depth, state, done, metrics Execute action get_state (none) state (STATE) Query position/heading episode_info (none) instruction (TEXT), episode_id (TEXT) Get episode metadata panorama (none) composite (IMAGE), scene (TEXT) 360-degree view After either approach, these nodes appear in the canvas sidebar and can be wired into agent graphs. 3. The NodeSet (one file, both ways) Write one BaseNodeSet with 5 tool classes. This file is used by both Way 1 and Way 2 — no changes needed between them. This listing is the simplified pedagogical version. The production workspace/nodesets/env/env_habitat.py is ~1700 lines and follows a 3-layer split: (1) HabitatEnvManager singleton (raw sim + GL-thread executor) · (2) thin BaseCanvasNode tool adapters (observe, step, localize, episode_info, navigate, panorama, frontiers, query_map) · (3) EnvHabitatNodeSet (lifecycle + ClassVars + env panel registration). Read the file alongside this tutorial; the extras the tutorial doesn't show are in § 5.3. workspace/nodesets/env/env_habitat.py """Habitat environment as a NodeSet (``EnvHabitatNodeSet``, name="env_habitat"). Works both as a local in-process nodeset and as an auto-hosted server. Load locally: POST /api/components/nodesets/env_habitat/load Load as server: POST /api/components/nodesets/env_habitat/load?mode=server """ from __future__ import annotations import asyncio import logging import os from typing import Any import numpy as np from app.components import BaseCanvasNode , BaseNodeSet , PortDef log = logging . getLogger ( __name__ ) # ── Helper ── def _get_env_mgr (): """Singleton accessor. The real ``HabitatEnvManager`` is defined in this same file (above) — wrapping the raw Habitat-Sim API with a threading.Lock and a single-thread ThreadPoolExecutor that pins all simulator work to one OS thread (GL / physics affinity). """ return HabitatEnvManager . get () async def _run_blocking ( fn , * args ): """Run a blocking function in the env manager's executor.""" mgr = _get_env_mgr () loop = asyncio . get_running_loop () return await loop . run_in_executor ( mgr . _executor , fn , * args ) def _extract_rgb_depth ( raw ): """Extract rgb and depth ndarrays from raw Habitat observations.""" rgb = depth = None if raw : for k in ( "rgb" , "RGB" ): if k in raw : rgb = np . asarray ( raw [ k ], dtype = np . uint8 ) break for k in ( "depth" , "DEPTH" ): if k in raw : depth = np . asarray ( raw [ k ], dtype = np . float32 ) . squeeze () break return rgb , depth # ── Tool Nodes ── class HabitatObserve ( BaseCanvasNode ): node_type = "habitat_observe" display_name = "Habitat: Observe" description = "Get current RGB + depth without stepping" category = "environment" icon = "Eye" input_ports = [] output_ports = [ PortDef ( "rgb" , "IMAGE" , "Current RGB observation" ), PortDef ( "depth" , "DEPTH" , "Current depth map" ), ] async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : mgr = _get_env_mgr () raw = await _run_blocking ( mgr . get_raw_obs ) rgb , depth = _extract_rgb_depth ( raw ) return { "rgb" : rgb , "depth" : depth } class HabitatStep ( BaseCanvasNode ): node_type = "habitat_step" display_name = "Habitat: Step" description = "Execute a navigation action" category = "environment" icon = "Play" input_ports = [ PortDef ( "action" , "ACTION" , "Action to execute (0-3)" ), ] output_ports = [ PortDef ( "rgb" , "IMAGE" , "Post-step RGB" ), PortDef ( "depth" , "DEPTH" , "Post-step depth" ), PortDef ( "state" , "STATE" , "Post-step agent state" ), PortDef ( "action" , "ACTION" , "Echo of executed action" ), PortDef ( "done" , "BOOL" , "Whether episode ended" ), PortDef ( "metrics" , "METRICS" , "Final metrics (when done)" ), ] async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : mgr = _get_env_mgr () action = int ( inputs . get ( "action" , 1 )) result = await _run_blocking ( mgr . step , action ) raw = await _run_blocking ( mgr . get_raw_obs ) rgb , depth = _extract_rgb_depth ( raw ) return { "rgb" : rgb , "depth" : depth , "state" : { "position" : result . get ( "position" , [ 0 , 0 , 0 ]), "orientation" : result . get ( "orientation" , [ 0 , 0 , 0 , 1 ]), }, "action" : action , "done" : result . get ( "done" , False ), "metrics" : result . get ( "metrics" ), } class HabitatState ( BaseCanvasNode ): node_type = "habitat_state" display_name = "Habitat: State" description = "Get agent position and orientation" category = "environment" icon = "MapPin" input_ports = [] output_ports = [ PortDef ( "state" , "STATE" , "Agent position + heading" ), ] async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : mgr = _get_env_mgr () state = await _run_blocking ( mgr . get_state ) return { "state" : state } class HabitatEpisodeInfo ( BaseCanvasNode ): node_type = "habitat_episode_info" display_name = "Habitat: Episode Info" description = "Get current episode instruction and ID" category = "environment" icon = "Info" input_ports = [] output_ports = [ PortDef ( "instruction" , "TEXT" , "Navigation instruction" ), PortDef ( "episode_id" , "TEXT" , "Episode ID" ), ] async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : mgr = _get_env_mgr () info = await _run_blocking ( mgr . get_episode_info ) return { "instruction" : info . get ( "instruction" , "" ), "episode_id" : str ( info . get ( "episode_id" , "" )), } class HabitatPanorama ( BaseCanvasNode ): node_type = "habitat_panorama" display_name = "Habitat: Panorama" description = "Render 360-degree panoramic observation" category = "environment" icon = "Scan" input_ports = [] output_ports = [ PortDef ( "composite" , "IMAGE" , "Composite panorama image" ), PortDef ( "scene" , "TEXT" , "View direction descriptions" ), ] async def forward ( self , inputs : dict , ctx : Any ) -> dict : mgr = _get_env_mgr () n_views = self . config . get ( "n_views" , 4 ) pano = await _run_blocking ( mgr . render_panorama , n_views ) composite = None b64 = pano . get ( "composite_base64" , "" ) if b64 : import base64 , io from PIL import Image buf = io . BytesIO ( base64 . b64decode ( b64 )) composite = np . array ( Image . open ( buf ) . convert ( "RGB" ), dtype = np . uint8 ) scene = " \n " . join ( " %s : heading %s deg" % ( v [ "direction" ], v [ "heading_deg" ]) for v in pano . get ( "views" , []) ) return { "composite" : composite , "scene" : scene } # ── NodeSet ── class EnvHabitatNodeSet ( BaseNodeSet ): name = "env_habitat" description = "Habitat-Sim VLN-CE environment" # Habitat-Sim is binary-locked to Python 3.8, so this defaults the # auto-hosted server to the `vlnce` conda env. server_python = os . environ . get ( "VLNCE_PYTHON" , "python" ) async def initialize ( self , ** kwargs ): """Initialize the…

title: Tutorials
text: Tutorials Step-by-step guides for building on AgentCanvas. Available Tutorials Component Cookbook — Comprehensive, progressive tutorial covering all customizable components: nodes, nodesets, config fields, UI customization, display fields, logging, dynamic ports, server mode, graphs, composites, dataflow loops, state containers, and policy wrapping. Start here. Creating a Habitat NodeSet — Wrap the Habitat-Sim environment as a NodeSet, shown both as a local nodeset (same process) and a server nodeset (separate process). Covers BaseNodeSet , ServerNodeSet , ServerApp , wire types, and the full file layout. Using the Execution Log System — Add voluntary logging to custom nodes, query logs via REST API, read JSONL files, and view results in the frontend Log Viewer. Includes a standalone runnable script and a complete example nodeset. Driving the Backend from a Coding Agent — Three ways to make a coding agent (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) drive batch evals on AgentCanvas: /architect:experiment-http (legacy skill, HTTP + bash spawn), the raw MCP server (typed tools, conversational), and /architect:experiment-mcp (default skill — iter-tree bookkeeping over MCP). Side-by-side comparison and when to combine them. Section contents batch eval 📄 coding agent backend 📄 component cookbook 📄 execution logs 📄 habitat nodeset 📄 contributing a nodeset 📄 writing a search operator 📄

title: Running an Architecture Search
text: Running an Architecture Search Agent Architecture Search (AAS) on AgentCanvas runs through the /architect:<variant>:loop slash-skill family — seven variants sharing a common iter-tree + evaluator contract. Each variant is a methodology (ADAS-style sub-agent Reflexion, AFlow-style MCTS, knowledge-distillation, etc.) and exposes the same five-skill surface: understand → proposer → implementer → evaluator → loop . This tutorial shows how to run a search, then how to fork a variant when you need a new search algorithm. Variant family vs the planned Python plugin API. Today's AAS surface is the variant family — Claude-conversation drivers, fast iteration on prompts / lineage / shape. A second surface — a Python BaseSearchOperator plugin API — is designed and waiting to be built (roadmap F8–F12). The two are intended to coexist; see Design — Search Operator API (RFC) for the planned plugin contract and how variants would map onto it. This tutorial covers today's working path. 1. Prerequisites Backend up at :8000 with /experiment:run reachable (see Driving the Backend § Mode A ). A seed graph at workspace/graphs/{name}.json plus a per-graph performance profile workspace/architect/exp_profiles/{name}.yaml (the variant's evaluator auto-bootstraps conservative defaults if it's missing). An experiment profile in .claude/experiment/profiles.yaml matching your workload's VRAM / GPU declaration. Read the chosen variant's README.md before invoking — they have different contracts and iter-dir conventions (see the Required reading stanza at the top of each loop.md ). 2. The Variant Family Variant One-line Methodology heritage adas-subagent One iter = one Claude conversation; 3 Reflexion calls become sub-agent spawns for sampling diversity ADAS (Hu et al. 2024) reframed for the coding-agent era aflow MCTS over workflows; tree policy + backprop on operator instance AFlow (Zhang et al. 2024) myloop Knowledge-distillation orchestrator — hard THINK → EXPERIMENT → DISTILL triple per iter; no archive, 7-file working memory per schemas.md Distillation-oriented variant All three live under .claude/commands/architect/<variant>/ . Pick the one whose contract matches your search shape: You want… Use An archive-based AAS loop with LLM proposing graph patches against top-K parents adas-subagent MCTS / tree-search over workflows aflow Knowledge-distillation pipeline (think + experiment + distill per iter) myloop 3. Anatomy of a Variant Every variant has the same five-skill surface plus a few accessories. The standard set: .claude/commands/architect/<variant>/ ├── README.md # core contracts + framing note + library/data ├── loop.md # orchestrator: owns the iter conversation, │ # drives proposer → implementer → evaluator ├── understand.md # one-shot pre-search briefing for the orchestrator ├── proposer.md # generate the next patch (mutation / proposal) ├── implementer.md # apply patch + smoke-test it (Tier-1 eval) ├── evaluator.md # delegate to _common/evaluator.md for Tier-2 perf eval ├── config.yaml # variant config: perf_profile_key, evaluator settings, │ # variant notes, side-effect artifacts └── lib/ # variant-specific Python helpers (atomic commit, # lineage rendering, archive maintenance, etc.) What's shared across all variants lives in _common/ : .claude/commands/architect/_common/ ├── files-contract.md # universal run-dir layout, edit whitelist, │ # resolve protocol, per-graph exp.yaml schema ├── evaluator.md # Tier-2 perf eval + bootstrap_CI (verbatim from │ # ADAS upstream utils.py:31-76) └── implementer.md # generic implementer routine (variants stub their # evaluator.md to call _common's) The two-tier evaluation: Tier 1 — Smoke (5 eps, in implementer.md ) — gates entry to performance eval. If smoke crashes, the iter aborts before paying the Tier 2 cost. Tier 2 — Performance (100 eps + bootstrap CI, in _common/evaluator.md ) — runs once after implementer succeeds, writes staged metrics with confidence intervals. This split makes variants cheap to fork (you swap proposer.md and maybe implementer.md, the evaluator is shared) and audits straightforward (the bootstrap CI algorithm is byte-identical across all variants). 4. Your First Search Run Pick a variant + a graph + a max-iter budget. The variant owns the per-iter Claude conversation; you watch. # ADAS-style 5-iter search on a NavGPT-MP3D seed /architect:adas-subagent:loop navgpt_mp3d --max-iters 5 # myloop with an explicit goal /architect:myloop:loop mapgpt_mp3d --goal "improve SR by 5pp" --max-iters 3 # Use a custom workspace overlay (e.g. an in-flight iter's active_workspace) /architect:adas-subagent:loop navgpt_mp3d --max-iters 3 \ --workspace=/path/to/vlnworkspace/outputs/design_runs/adas-subagent/navgpt_mp3d/v0/active_workspace The loop: Bootstrap — resolves (graph, vN, iter_M) ; if iter_0 doesn't exist, runs baseline perf eval directly (no proposer, no implementer) so the search has a reference point. Per iter : proposer reads top-K parents from archive (variant- dependent) and emits a patch under iter_M/diff/ → implementer applies it to a fresh active_workspace/ overlay and runs 5-ep smoke → if smoke passes, evaluator runs 100-ep perf eval via /experiment:run + bootstrap CI → metrics get committed atomically to iter_M/ . Atomic commit — failed iters do not partial-pollute the iter-tree; the variant's lib/helpers.py:atomic_commit ensures either everything lands or nothing does. Termination — --max-iters or variant-specific termination_check (e.g. myloop's goal.md § Termination ). The eval step itself goes through /experiment:run , which means it's subject to JobScheduler GPU admission + queueing. If a sibling session is holding GPU exclusively, your iter will wait at the evaluator step. See Running a Parallel Batch Eval for the eval-side knobs (worker count, per-step budget, episode count) — those are all set via the variant's config.yaml § evaluator + the graph's .exp.yaml . 5. Authoring a New Variant If none of the seven fits, fork the closest existing one — that's how the family grew: # Example: fork adas-subagent as a starting point for a new ADAS-shape variant. cp -r .claude/commands/architect/adas-subagent .claude/commands/architect/my-variant Then walk through the five skill files and edit what your methodology needs: File When to edit README.md Always — restate your variant's contracts + framing note. Variants that diverge from _common/files-contract.md must document the divergence here. loop.md When the per-iter conversation shape changes (e.g. you spawn sub-agents differently, gate on different signals, store different working memory). proposer.md Almost always — this is where your search algorithm lives (mutation strategy, parent selection, prompt template). implementer.md Rarely — usually stubs to _common/implementer.md . Edit only if your variant has a non-standard smoke contract. evaluator.md Rarely — the stub binds VARIANT + VARIANT_DIR and points at _common/evaluator.md . Edit only if you need a different bootstrap algorithm (you usually don't — verbatim ADAS algorithm is the agreed common ground). config.yaml Always — set perf_profile_key , evaluator settings, side-effect artifact paths. lib/ If you need custom atomic-commit, lineage rendering, or archive bookkeeping beyond what the variant you forked already provides. The hard contract you must preserve: the iter-dir layout ( outputs/design_runs/<variant>/<graph>/vN/iteration/iter_M/ ) and the files inside it (smoke / perf metrics, snapshot, lineage edges). Everything else is your call. The contract is enforced by _common/files-contract.md — read it before deviating. Smoke-test your new variant on a tiny budget first ( --max-iters 1 --episodes 5 ) before turning it loose on a real graph. 6. Output Layout outputs/design_runs/<variant>/<graph>/ └── vN/ # version line — bump for major method pivots ├── config.md # vN-level: methodology, baseline assumptions ├── trace.md # one row per iter — verdict + delta ├── lineage.md # parent → child edges…
